--- SOURCE 2: Controlling Complex Systems - arXiv ---
URL: https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1

SUMMARY:
Controlling complex systems typically means enforcing a specific collective behavior, ranging from consensus and synchronization to pattern

FULL CONTENT:
# Controlling Complex Systems

###### Abstract

This chapter provides a comprehensive overview of controlling collective behavior in complex systems comprising large ensembles of interacting dynamical agents. Building upon traditional control theory‚Äôs foundation in individual systems, we introduce tools designed to address the unique challenges of coordinating networks that exhibit emergent phenomena, including consensus, synchronization, and pattern formation.
We analyze how local agent interactions generate macroscopic behaviors and investigate the fundamental role of network topology in determining system dynamics.
Inspired by natural systems, we emphasize control strategies that achieve global coordination through localized interventions while considering practical implementation challenges. The chapter concludes by presenting novel frameworks for managing very large agent ensembles and leveraging interacting networks for control purposes.

Keywords: Complex systems; Networks; Large-scale systems; Multi-agent systems; Network controllability; Network control; Node control; Pinning control; Adaptive control; Edge control; Structural control; Stability.

Key points:

The most common formalism to model complex systems is that of a graph‚ÄĒwhere vertices represent agents and edges represent the interaction between them‚ÄĒwith agent dynamics modeled via ordinary differential equations.

Controlling complex systems typically means enforcing a specific collective behavior, ranging from consensus and synchronization to pattern formation and herding.

Control strategies for complex systems can be either centralized or distributed, and can often be categorized as either node control, edge control, or structural control.

Tools for establishing stability and convergence of control strategies include Lyapunov theory, contraction theory, and the master stability function approach.

###### Contents

## 1 Introduction and Motivation

Modern technological advances have fundamentally changed our perspective on feedback control.
While classical control theory has primarily focused on regulating individual dynamical systems, contemporary challenges increasingly involve coordinating and controlling large groups of interconnected systems.
From power distribution in smart grids to autonomous vehicle formations, from biological cell networks to social systems, we encounter scenarios where multiple agents must coordinate their behavior to achieve common objectives (Newman et¬†al. [2006](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib111), Strogatz [2001](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib141)).

Systems comprising multiple interacting units possess a distinctive characteristic that distinguishes them from traditional dynamical systems: they exhibit emergent collective behaviors that cannot be *easily* derived from the analysis of individual components. These systems, known as *complex systems*, represent a fundamental shift in our understanding of dynamics. This concept emerged in the 1940s (Weaver [1948](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib153)), and while its precise definition remains debated without complete consensus, scholars broadly agree that collective behavior arising from local interactions represents the fundamental characteristic of such systems (Vicsek [2002](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib147), Ladyman et¬†al. [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib87), Torres et¬†al. [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib144), Bianconi et¬†al. [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib18), Estrada [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib61)).
Moreover, the concept of interactions leading to emergent behavior is fundamentally linked to *feedback* in control theory (√Östr√∂m &¬†Murray [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib12)).
Indeed, complex systems can be viewed as networks of numerous feedback loops, their *degree of complexity* determined by both the number and intricacy of these interconnections.
These systems are inherently *multi-scale*, as microscopic interactions between agents generate macroscopic collective behaviors observable at the population level.

The simplest example of such collective behavior is consensus, where all agents converge to a common equilibrium state (Olfati-Saber et¬†al. [2007](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib116)).
A more general phenomenon, often observed in both natural and engineered systems, is synchronization, where an ensemble of interconnected nonlinear agents converges towards the same time-varying trajectory (Pikovsky et¬†al. [2001](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib121)).
Beyond these fundamental phenomena, collective behavior manifests in diverse forms including pattern formation, platooning, and clustering.

The development of effective strategies for controlling collective behavior of complex multi-agent systems has become increasingly critical in addressing contemporary challenges.
These include the transformation of traditional power systems into smart grids and energy communities (Dobson [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib53), Doerfler et¬†al. [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib54)), the engineering of resilient and sustainable supply chains (Hearnshaw &¬†Wilson [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib75)), the implementation of smart sensorized cities (Wang, Jiang, Zhang, Quek, Ren &¬†Hanzo [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib149)) and transportation networks (Alam et¬†al. [2015](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib3)), the advancement of synthetic biological control systems (Del¬†Vecchio et¬†al. [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib42)), and the evolution of coordinated swarm robotic systems (Annaswamy et¬†al. [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib8)).

The collective behavior emerging from large ensembles of interacting dynamical systems is strongly influenced by the structure of the interaction network among its constitutive agents (Liu &¬†Barab√°si [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib89)).
These networks typically diverge from simple regular lattices (Newman [2003](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib109), [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib110)), exhibiting intricate topological features that profoundly shape system behavior.
The interactions between agents further complicate the dynamics, as they can be time-varying, intermittent, subject to delays, or governed by nonlinear functions (Cort√©s [2006](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib39), Meng et¬†al. [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib101), Almeida et¬†al. [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib5)).

Natural systems provide compelling examples of efficient control strategies.
Biological systems, in particular, demonstrate how global coordination can emerge through the influence of a remarkably small number of agents (Chen et¬†al. [2014](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib31)).
This principle of achieving global control through localized actions on select agents holds profound implications for technological applications.
However, its implementation requires addressing fundamental questions: how to achieve and maintain desired collective behaviors using only local information, how to identify which agents to control and with what intensity, and how to formally demonstrate convergence to desired behaviors.
Building on these foundational challenges, many pressing applications, such as search and rescue operations (Queralta et¬†al. [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib123)) or traffic control (Siri et¬†al. [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib137)), require a group of controller agents to work together effectively in order to shape the behavior of a separate group of target agents. Recent research has focused on this idea of harnessing complex systems for control, emphasizing both the design of the controller populations and the specific conditions needed to successfully coordinate the target behaviors; a notable example is that of shepherding problems as discussed in Lama &¬†di¬†Bernardo ([2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib88)).

![Refer to caption](extracted/6350711/Figures/Encyclopedia_Figure1.png)

Practical implementation must address several critical challenges that arise in real-world applications.
Heterogeneity among agents presents a fundamental difficulty, particularly in biological applications where identical components are rare (Del¬†Vecchio et¬†al. [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib42)).
This is compounded by communication constraints and the reality of only having access to partial information about the system state (Hespanha et¬†al. [2007](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib76), Zhang et¬†al. [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib165)).
Environmental disturbances and measurement noise further complicate the control problem, while the dynamic nature of many networks leads to time-varying topologies and interaction patterns that must be handled robustly. These practical considerations represent significant hurdles that must be overcome to translate theoretical control strategies into effective real-world solutions.

An emerging frontier is the development of strategies for systems comprising an extremely large number of agents. In such scenarios, the classical description of complex systems, which specifies the dynamics and interactions of each individual agent, becomes both analytically and computationally intractable. This has led to the development of new frameworks that address these systems directly at the population level through continuum descriptions (Nikitin et¬†al. [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib113)).

This chapter presents a systematic exploration of the problem of controlling complex network systems. While the field is vast and rapidly evolving, we focus on control strategies that are general in their formulation and can be proved formally to converge. For broader perspectives on specific applications and alternative approaches, readers are referred to comprehensive reviews in both control theory and physics literature (Liu &¬†Barab√°si [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib89), Meyn [2007](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib103), Su &¬†Wang [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib142), D‚ÄôSouza et¬†al. [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib57)).
The subsequent sections develop these concepts systematically, moving from basic modeling frameworks to advanced control strategies. We examine both theoretical foundations and practical implementations, with particular attention to emerging applications and future challenges in this exciting field.

## 2 Modeling Complex Systems

Understanding and controlling complex systems requires a systematic modeling framework for the latter.
A complex system can be understood as being the ensemble of *units* (the atomic entities in the system) and the *interactions* between them.
The units are often called *systems*, *nodes*, or *agents*, while the the interactions are also referred to as *communication*, *(inter-)connections* and *links*.
Mathematically, a complex system can be modeled through three fundamental components (see Figure [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#S1.F1 "Figure 1 ‚Ä£ 1 Introduction and Motivation ‚Ä£ Controlling Complex Systems")):
(i) the dynamics of the agents,
(ii) the structure of their interconnections, and
(iii) their interaction protocols.
This description enables capturing both the microscopic behavior of individual agents and the emergence of macroscopic collective phenomena exhibited at a network level.
We expound each of the three components below.

### 2.1 Agent Dynamics

In most control problems, it is assumed that each agent has a state that can change in time (see Figure [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#S1.F1 "Figure 1 ‚Ä£ 1 Introduction and Motivation ‚Ä£ Controlling Complex Systems")b, right panel).
Several mathematical formalisms are used to describe such dynamics, including (ordinary, delay, stochastic, and partial) differential equations, difference equations, Markov decision processes, and probability density/mass functions; yet, the most common formalism is arguably that of ordinary differential equations.
In that case, each agent is described as a nonlinear affine dynamical system, i.e.,

fi:‚ĄĚn‚Üí‚ĄĚn:subscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĖ‚Üísuperscript‚ĄĚūĚĎõsuperscript‚ĄĚūĚĎõf\_{i}:\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R}^{n}italic\_f start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT : blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_n end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ‚Üí blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_n end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT is the *agent*‚Äôs (also known as *individual*, *internal* or *intrinsic*) *dynamics*,
vi‚ĀĘ(t)‚ąą‚ĄĚmsubscriptūĚĎ£ūĚĎĖūĚĎ°superscript‚ĄĚūĚĎöv\_{i}(t)\in\mathbb{R}^{m}italic\_v start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_t ) ‚ąą blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_m end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT is the input coming from the agent‚Äôs *neighbors*‚ÄĒi.e., other agents with whom it is connected‚ÄĒGi:‚ĄĚn‚Üí‚ĄĚn√óm:subscriptūĚźļūĚĎĖ‚Üísuperscript‚ĄĚūĚĎõsuperscript‚ĄĚūĚĎõūĚĎöG\_{i}:\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}italic\_G start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT : blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_n end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ‚Üí blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_n √ó italic\_m end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT describes how the agent responds to interaction,
and N‚ąą‚ĄēūĚĎĀ‚ĄēN\in\mathbb{N}italic\_N ‚ąą blackboard\_N is the number of agents in the network.

The choice of the agent dynamics fisubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĖf\_{i}italic\_f start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT depends heavily on the application, with the complexity of the mathematical model varying according to the specific context.
In the simplest case, agents can be modeled as simple or higher-order integrators (Ren &¬†Beard [2008](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib126)).
However, often more sophisticated models are required to capture the key dynamical features of the system.
For example, autonomous vehicle applications generally involve second-order motion dynamics to capture both position and velocity states (Ye &¬†Yamamoto [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib161)); in power systems, agents are represented by nonlinear oscillators describing power generators that must maintain synchronization (Hill &¬†Chen [2006](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib77)); on the other hand, biological networks present arguably one of the most challenging scenarios, where agents represent complex nonlinear and stochastic cellular processes with intricate interaction patterns.
Examples of applications modeled via different frameworks include gene regulatory networks, often described using Markov chains or stochastic differential equations (Gillespie [2000](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib68)),
systems of faults, modeled via discontinuous dynamical systems (Burridge &¬†Knopoff [1967](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib29)) and communication networks, typically represented through difference equations (Zhu &¬†Mart√≠nez [2010](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib169)).
The choice of appropriate agent dynamics modeling is context-dependent and must strike a good trade-off between model fidelity and analytical tractability.

### 2.2 Network Structure

The second essential component of a complex system is the *network structure*, which describes the interconnections between agents.
Most commonly, interactions are assumed to be *pair-wise*, i.e., each one involving exactly two agents.
In this case, the structure is represented by a *graph* (Godsil &¬†Royle [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib70)), say ūĚíĘ=(ūĚíĪ,‚Ąį)ūĚíĘūĚíĪ‚Ąį\mathcal{G}=(\mathcal{V},\mathcal{E})caligraphic\_G = ( caligraphic\_V , caligraphic\_E ), where ūĚíĪ={1,‚Ä¶,N}ūĚíĪ1‚Ä¶ūĚĎĀ\mathcal{V}=\{1,\dots,N\}caligraphic\_V = { 1 , ‚Ä¶ , italic\_N } is the set of *vertices* (the agents), and ‚Ąį‚äÜūĚíĪ√óūĚíĪ‚ĄįūĚíĪūĚíĪ\mathcal{E}\subseteq\mathcal{V}\times\mathcal{V}caligraphic\_E ‚äÜ caligraphic\_V √ó caligraphic\_V is the set of *edges* (the interconnections), see Figure [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#S1.F1 "Figure 1 ‚Ä£ 1 Introduction and Motivation ‚Ä£ Controlling Complex Systems")b, left panel.
Here we assumed an *undirected* graph (the presence of a connection from agent iūĚĎĖiitalic\_i to jūĚĎójitalic\_j also implies a connection from jūĚĎójitalic\_j to iūĚĎĖiitalic\_i), but *directed* graphs are also common; in *weighted* graphs‚ÄĒas opposed to the *unweighted* ones assumed here‚ÄĒeach edge also has a scalar value associated to it, typically representing a meaningful quantity in the application of interest (importance, capacity, travel time, etc.).

Graphs can be represented algebraically through two matrices.
The first one is the *adjacency matrix* A‚ąą{0,1}N√óNūĚźīsuperscript01ūĚĎĀūĚĎĀA\in\{0,1\}^{N\times N}italic\_A ‚ąą { 0 , 1 } start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_N √ó italic\_N end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, with Ai‚ĀĘj=1subscriptūĚźīūĚĎĖūĚĎó1A\_{ij}=1italic\_A start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 1 if an edge from iūĚĎĖiitalic\_i to jūĚĎójitalic\_j is present and 00 otherwise.
Note that different authors can adopt different conventions on whether such edge means that agent iūĚĎĖiitalic\_i influences jūĚĎójitalic\_j or that iūĚĎĖiitalic\_i is influenced by jūĚĎójitalic\_j.
The second matrix is the *graph Laplacian matrix* L‚ąą‚Ą§N√óNūĚźŅsuperscript‚Ą§ūĚĎĀūĚĎĀL\in\mathbb{Z}^{N\times N}italic\_L ‚ąą blackboard\_Z start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_N √ó italic\_N end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, given by L=D‚ąíAūĚźŅūĚź∑ūĚźīL=D-Aitalic\_L = italic\_D - italic\_A, where D‚ąą‚ĄēN√óNūĚź∑superscript‚ĄēūĚĎĀūĚĎĀD\in\mathbb{N}^{N\times N}italic\_D ‚ąą blackboard\_N start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_N √ó italic\_N end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT is the *degree matrix*, which is diagonal and with Di‚ĀĘisubscriptūĚź∑ūĚĎĖūĚĎĖD\_{ii}italic\_D start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT equaling the *degree* of node iūĚĎĖiitalic\_i, i.e., the number of connections involving agent iūĚĎĖiitalic\_i (Newman [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib110)).
Network graphs can also be characterized macroscopically via a wide variety of descriptors, such as the *average degree*, the *clustering coefficient*, *path lengths*, *diameter*, different *node centrality measures* and *community structure*; for detailed descriptions, see Boccaletti et¬†al. ([2006](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib21)), Newman ([2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib110)).

Elementary well-known graphs typically used for model validation include *paths*, *rings*, *stars*, wheels,
*complete*, and kūĚĎėkitalic\_k-nearest neighbors (Godsil &¬†Royle [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib70)).
On the other hand, it has been found that in applications certain kinds of structures tend to appear more consistently.
These include
*random graphs* (built via the ErdŇĎs-R√©nyi model (ErdŇĎs &¬†R√©nyi [1959](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib59))),
*lattices*, that can be drawn as regular tilings,
*small-world networks*, characterized by high clustering and short path lengths (Wieland et¬†al. [2011](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib154)),
*scale-free networks* exhibiting power-law degree distributions (Watts &¬†Strogatz [1998](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib152), Liu &¬†Barab√°si [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib89)), and hierarchical or modular structures (Ravasz et¬†al. [2002](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib125)).

In the recent years, two more advanced modeling frameworks gained traction: multilayer networks, and networks with higher-order interactions.
In *multilayer networks*, multiple graphs are used to describe the network; connections between agents in different graphs are possible, and if the vertices sets are the same for all graphs, the network is said to be *multiplex* (Bianconi [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib17)).
This formalism is used for example to describe multimodal transportation networks, where each mode of transportation (e.g., buses, subway, taxis) is modeled via a different graph, or social networks where each platform (e.g., Facebook, Instagram, X) is a graph, and more (Boccaletti et¬†al. [2014](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib19)).
Networks with interactions that involve more than two agents (*higher order interactions*) are formalized via *hypergraphs*.
These networks model, for instance, scientific authorship and biological processes Boccaletti et¬†al. ([2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib20)).
Special cases of hypergraphs are *simplicial complexes*, where the presence of a connection between a certain number of agents implies the presence of all possible connections between any proper subset of these agents.

### 2.3 Interaction Protocols

The third key element is the protocol through which agents exchange information and influence each other over existing connections (see Figure [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#S1.F1 "Figure 1 ‚Ä£ 1 Introduction and Motivation ‚Ä£ Controlling Complex Systems")b, right panel).
Assuming pair-wise (unweighted) interactions, a common mathematical description for the interaction term visubscriptūĚĎ£ūĚĎĖv\_{i}italic\_v start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT in ([1](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#S2.E1 "In 2.1 Agent Dynamics ‚Ä£ 2 Modeling Complex Systems ‚Ä£ Controlling Complex Systems")) is

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | vi=ŌÉ‚ĀĘ‚ąĎj=1NAi‚ĀĘj‚ĀĘh‚ĀĘ(xi,xj),subscriptūĚĎ£ūĚĎĖūĚúésuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎó1ūĚĎĀsubscriptūĚźīūĚĎĖūĚĎó‚ĄésubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎóv\_{i}=\sigma\sum\_{j=1}^{N}A\_{ij}h(x\_{i},x\_{j}),italic\_v start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic\_ŌÉ ‚ąĎ start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j = 1 end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_N end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_A start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_h ( italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) , |  | (2) |

where ŌÉ‚ąą‚ĄĚūĚúé‚ĄĚ\sigma\in\mathbb{R}italic\_ŌÉ ‚ąą blackboard\_R represents the *coupling* (or *interaction*) *strength* (or *gain*), Ai‚ĀĘj‚ąą{0,1}subscriptūĚźīūĚĎĖūĚĎó01A\_{ij}\in\{0,1\}italic\_A start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ‚ąą { 0 , 1 } is the (i,j)ūĚĎĖūĚĎó(i,j)( italic\_i , italic\_j )-th element of the adjacency matrix, and h‚ĀĘ(xi,xj):‚ĄĚn√ó‚ĄĚn‚Üí‚ĄĚm:‚ĄésubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎó‚Üísuperscript‚ĄĚūĚĎõsuperscript‚ĄĚūĚĎõsuperscript‚ĄĚūĚĎöh(x\_{i},x\_{j}):\mathbb{R}^{n}\times\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R}^{m}italic\_h ( italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) : blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_n end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT √ó blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_n end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ‚Üí blackboard\_R start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_m end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT describes the *interaction protocol*.

The interaction protocol is *diffusive* if it can be written as h‚ĀĘ(xj‚ąíxi)‚ĄésubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎósubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎĖh(x\_{j}-x\_{i})italic\_h ( italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ).
In particular, a simple and common choice for h‚Ąéhitalic\_h is a *linear diffusive coupling*, i.e., h‚ĀĘ(xi,xj)=xj‚ąíxi‚ĄésubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎósubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎósubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎĖh(x\_{i},x\_{j})=x\_{j}-x\_{i}italic\_h ( italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT.
However, real systems often exhibit more complex interaction patterns that go well beyond simple linear protocols.
Time delays inevitably arise in communication or actuation (Hespanha et¬†al. [2007](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib76), Zhang et¬†al. [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib165)), while coupling strengths between agents are rarely uniform, requiring heterogeneous gains (ŌÉi‚ĀĘjsubscriptūĚúéūĚĎĖūĚĎó\sigma\_{ij}italic\_ŌÉ start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT instead of a single ŌÉūĚúé\sigmaitalic\_ŌÉ) to accurately represent the varying connection strengths [e.g., Simpson-Porco et¬†al. ([2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib136))].
Also, the interaction strengths can evolve dynamically based on the states of the connected nodes through adaptive coupling laws (DeLellis, di¬†Bernardo &¬†Garofalo [2009](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib43)).
Further complexity arises from intermittent or switching interactions, where connections between agents may be temporarily lost or deliberately modified, or instantaneously commutating between different values (Yang et¬†al. [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib158), Coraggio et¬†al. [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib35)).
These realistic features must be carefully considered when designing control strategies for practical applications.

### 2.4 Simplified Network Model

While real systems exhibit considerable complexity, analysis often begins with simplifying assumptions. These typically include
(i) considering identical agents (fi=fj=fsubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎóūĚĎďf\_{i}=f\_{j}=fitalic\_f start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic\_f start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic\_f, ‚ąÄi,j

for-allūĚĎĖūĚĎó\forall i,j‚ąÄ italic\_i , italic\_j) ,
(ii) unitary response to interaction (Gi=IsubscriptūĚźļūĚĎĖūĚźľG\_{i}=Iitalic\_G start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic\_I, ‚ąÄifor-allūĚĎĖ\forall i‚ąÄ italic\_i),
(iii) static pair-wise interactions,
and (iv) a linear diffusive coupling protocol (h‚ĀĘ(xi,xj)=xj‚ąíxi‚ĄésubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎósubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎósubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎĖh(x\_{i},x\_{j})=x\_{j}-x\_{i}italic\_h ( italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT).
Under these assumptions, the network dynamics can be written compactly as

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | xňôi‚ĀĘ(t)=f‚ĀĘ(xi‚ĀĘ(t))‚ąíŌÉ‚ĀĘ‚ąĎj=1NLi‚ĀĘj‚ĀĘxj‚ĀĘ(t),i‚ąą{1,‚Ä¶,N},formulae-sequencesubscriptňôūĚĎ•ūĚĎĖūĚĎ°ūĚĎďsubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎĖūĚĎ°ūĚúésuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎó1ūĚĎĀsubscriptūĚźŅūĚĎĖūĚĎósubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎóūĚĎ°ūĚĎĖ1‚Ä¶ūĚĎĀ\dot{x}\_{i}(t)=f(x\_{i}(t))-\sigma\sum\_{j=1}^{N}L\_{ij}x\_{j}(t),\quad i\in\{1,% \ldots,N\},overňô start\_ARG italic\_x end\_ARG start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_t ) = italic\_f ( italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_t ) ) - italic\_ŌÉ ‚ąĎ start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j = 1 end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_N end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic\_L start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic\_t ) , italic\_i ‚ąą { 1 , ‚Ä¶ , italic\_N } , |  | (3) |

where Li‚ĀĘjsubscriptūĚźŅūĚĎĖūĚĎóL\_{ij}italic\_L start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_i italic\_j end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT are the elements of the graph Laplacian matrix.
This simplified model, while not capturing all the complexities of real systems, provides a tractable starting point for theoretical analysis and has proven remarkably useful in understanding fundamental properties of networked systems (Bullo et¬†al. [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib25)).

![Refer to caption](extracted/6350711/Figures/Encyclopedia_Figure2.png)

## 3 Controlling Complex Systems

### 3.1 Control Goals

When controlling a complex system, we typically wish to enforce *convergence* (Pavlov et¬†al. [2004](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib119)) of the agents‚Äô states to a specific collective behavior.
One of the simplest ones is *consensus*, where all agents converge to the same constant state (Olfati-Saber et¬†al. [2007](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib116));
this can be desired in rendez-vous problems of mobile agents, sensor fusion problems, and can occur in opinion dynamics networks (Amirkhani &¬†Barshooi [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib6)).
*Synchronization* is arguably the most studied collective behavior and possibly the first being investigated, with Christiaan Huygens writing in 1673 about an ‚Äúodd kind of sympathy‚ÄĚ when he observed in the behavior of coupled pendulum clocks.
A synchronized complex system is one where all agents‚Äô states converge to the same solution, which can also be a non-constant function of time (Pikovsky et¬†al. [2001](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib121), Arenas et¬†al. [2008](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib10), Boccaletti et¬†al. [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib22)).
In power grids, synchronization of the derivatives of power angles associated to generators is a crucial requirement for correct operation (D√∂rfler &¬†Bullo [2014](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07579v1#bib.bib56)).
In the brain, moderate levels of neural synchronization can be desired, as it is linked to motor coordination, whereas excessive levels of synchronization are related to seizures (Po

FULL CONTENT:
![Case Western Reserve University](https://assets.everspringpartners.com/b8/9d/37e22a774e6da1a078ae1da2caae/cwru-logo-white.svg)

# Advanced Systems and Control Engineering: Shaping the Future of Technology

![Central control room of nuclear power plant](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNDZweCIgd2lkdGg9IjgwMHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=)

Artificial intelligence (AI) and [automation applications](https://online-engineering.case.edu/blog/the-role-of-systems-control-engineers-in-robotics-and-automation) are experiencing explosive growth in modern technology across all industries. Advanced systems and control engineering works at the intersection of operational technology and information technology, making it an important feature in many of these new applications.

Systems and control engineers design the regulators that control the behavior of a device or process. A simple example of this type of technology is the thermostat in your home. A sensor detects the temperature in your house and automatically adjusts the heating or cooling system until it reaches the temperature you‚Äôve set.1 Advanced systems and control engineering includes much more complicated projects, such as missile guidance systems, distribution and robotics, manufacturing facilities and power generation.1

Read on to explore how advanced control engineering is helping to shape the future of technology.

## Advanced Control Engineering

A control system controls a physical process. It receives an input, processes it, and then generates an appropriate output. Control engineering involves designing, analyzing, optimizing and implementing control systems so that they operate safely and at their best.2

Systems and control engineers use feedback loops to actuate systems. In open-loop systems, the output is not used to control the system. In closed-loop systems, the output is used to adjust the input. All control engineering systems are either open- or closed-loop.2

Engineers use various techniques to control these systems:2

## Systems Engineering Innovations

Systems engineering is a multidisciplinary approach that focuses on designing, integrating and managing complex systems from conception through retirement. In this context, a system includes all of the components that function together‚ÄĒsuch as hardware, software, facilities, personnel, processes and procedures‚ÄĒto meet a need.3

Systems engineers have developed innovative approaches to engineering, including model-based systems engineering (MBSE) and systems of systems (SoS).

### Model-Based Systems Engineering

In contrast to the text-based and manual processes used by traditional engineers, systems engineers often use digital models and simulations to create systems. MBSE is capable of providing visual and interactive models of complex systems. It represents the components of the system as well as the connections between them.4

The interactive nature of MBSE makes it more accurate and efficient than earlier methods. Each model serves as a single source of truth and provides traceability. [All relevant stakeholders](https://online-engineering.case.edu/blog/the-importance-of-collaboration-in-systems-control-engineering) work with the model and, therefore, the same information. Changes are immediately reflected in the model and widely distributed.4

Working with a virtual model makes it significantly easier to make changes and correct issues. This efficiency reduces the time and costs associated with developing new systems. This is particularly important for creating complex systems that are comprised of smaller systems. The MBSE can graphically illustrate relationships and dependencies within and between systems.4

### System of Systems

In a system of systems (SoS), multiple independent systems work together to form a larger, more complex system that can achieve results that no single one of the smaller systems can achieve on its own.5 Examples of common SoS include transportation and the power grid. The U.S. Department of Defense (DoD) also widely uses SoS to highlight spontaneous interconnection between systems that didn't previously appear related to one other.5

## Emerging Technologies in Control Systems

[New technologies](https://online-engineering.case.edu/blog/adding-new-technologies-to-existing-systems) are driving exciting innovations in control systems. These rapid advancements provide systems and control engineers with opportunities and challenges alike.

### Advanced Technologies

AI is a major aspect of new control system technologies. Autonomous control systems can operate for an extended period of time without human intervention. [AI and machine learning](https://online-engineering.case.edu/blog/advancements-in-artificial-intelligence-and-machine-learning) enable modern autonomous control systems to learn from experience and make improvements based on what they‚Äôve learned.6

Advancements in wireless technology are behind networked control systems. In these systems, sensing and actuation [signals are transmitted](https://online-engineering.case.edu/blog/signal-processing-control-systems-techniques-trends) throughout a system or multiple systems over a shared network. Networked systems often rely heavily on control systems embedded in devices connected to the Internet of Things (IoT). These systems allow organizations to track and manage complicated systems‚ÄĒsuch as inventory‚ÄĒeasily, at every stage of the supply chain.7

Cyber-physical systems integrate the cyber and physical world through computation, control, analytics and networking. Examples of cyber-physical systems include medical devices that monitor patient health and dispense medication, [smart buildings that optimize energy efficiency](https://online-engineering.case.edu/blog/building-smart-cities-and-infrastructure) and human comfort, and smart manufacturing facilities that improve productivity.8

### Challenges and Opportunities

Despite the promise of new technology that has emerged from control engineering research, there are significant challenges in the field, including cybersecurity, complexity and interoperability.

#### Cybersecurity

Cyber threats and cybersecurity attacks are an increasing concern in all industries. Due to their complexity, advanced control systems can open up increased attack surfaces. Hackers can use unsecured components of networked systems to obtain access to sensitive data and critical systems. Engineers are using new technology to improve security through AI, machine learning and data analytics.9

#### Complexity

Today‚Äôs systems are more complex than ever and often include numerous disciplines, hardware, software and stakeholders. As this complexity continues to grow, it becomes more difficult to manage the systems. Model-based systems engineering methods are helping engineers model and simulate these complex environments more realistically, in order to optimize them.10

#### Interoperability

Interoperability refers to the ability of a system‚Äôs components to communicate with each other and exchange data. The more components there are, the more likely engineers are to face interoperability issues. To ensure interoperability, engineers have to develop shared protocols and data storage within a system.11

enriched_points.expanded_content: Control systems are essential for managing complex engineering tasks, enabling automated responses to inputs and maintaining desired outputs through feedback mechanisms.
Robotics applies complex systems and control theory to design autonomous agents capable of navigating environments, performing tasks, and adapting to dynamic conditions.
Engineering systems must exhibit resilience, allowing them to maintain performance despite unexpected disturbances, ensuring reliability and stability in operations.
